🟡 Diamante Barú Bioregional Consortium

Case Study
Y

33314415-10e1-81f3-b0e6-fcc0d77bccf3, 33314415-10e1-8180-8414-e1dc3b99eb9a, 33314415-10e1-812a-80f5-ea9638877258, 33314415-10e1-8199-b89c-ce879c1935a0, 33314415-10e1-819d-85a3-ebd54cf13f4b
network_affiliation
[[BioFi Project]]
background_section
## Background Water defines the Diamante Barú region. From Chirripó at [3,820 meters above sea level](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE), it falls, infiltrates, and returns at the Diamante waterfall—[about 650 feet high, Central America’s tallest, rising as a spring from the ground](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE). It moves through the Térraba–Sierpe wetlands, Costa Rica’s largest mangrove system, and reaches Marino Ballena National Park, where formations create a “whale’s tail.” There, currents meet and split—[the only place where coastal currents run both north and south year-round](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE). In a very small area sits extraordinary diversity: [roughly half a million species—about 5% of global biodiversity—on 0.03% of Earth’s land](https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc). Corcovado National Park alone holds [about 2.5% of the world’s biodiversity](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE), tied to wetlands, rivers, and the agricultural basin below Chirripó around Pérez Zeledón. That richness sits alongside sharp threats. The basin is largely pasture yet leads the world in [per-capita use of pesticides and petrochemical fertilizers](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE); runoff moves through the watershed toward wetlands and the Osa Peninsula. For Indigenous nations—including the Boruca, Térraba, Bribrí, and Ngöbe—[more than half of their territories are now occupied by non-Indigenous settlers](https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc). ### Origins and Network Danny Bell co-founded the Diamante Bridge Collective in 2019 to channel crypto philanthropy toward grassroots regenerative work that often stays invisible—making [care for the earth a regenerative livelihood](https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc). The core team describes itself as ["a network of ambassadors… in service to this shared mission of bio-regioning (the verb)."](https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc) Because the Diamante Barú region borders the Osa Peninsula, they see ["this fractal element going on here."](https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc) [Eight Indigenous peoples hold territories covering about 7% of Costa Rica](https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc), all under extractive pressure.
Sources
33314415-10e1-8167-8e7d-fab2bddf992e, 33314415-10e1-8153-84d6-fa1880723484, 33314415-10e1-81f9-bd3f-d37db6923636
patterns

place

activities
Diamante Luz|RECenter the Resource Recycling Center San Salvador|Sacred Shield Initiative|Participatory Governance Development|Diamante Bioregional Trust Development
description
The Diamante Barú Bioregional Consortium unites a diverse collective of community leaders, Indigenous allies, and regenerative projects in Costa Rica's southern region. Their focus is on land rematriation, water protection, and grassroots bioregional governance.
vision_legacy_section
## Vision and Legacy The consortium frames its purpose in participants’ own words: ["We're co-starting a living system of regeneration, and we have the opportunity to protect territory and unify cultures by shifting capital today to fuel a decentralized movement restoring nature and rights with us."](https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc) Day to day, that means making ["care for the earth a regenerative livelihood"](https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc)—not a slogan but work supported by decentralized, transparent systems that pay grassroots effort. Indigenous knowledge and contemporary tools—cryptocurrency, sociocracy, monitoring—are brought together to defend a hyper-biodiverse region by trusting people who already know the watershed and are doing the work. The bet is direct: capital can reach grassroots action; governance can follow Indigenous protocols and community consensus; and protecting “this tiny little space on Earth” is protecting ["2.5% of the world's biodiversity"](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE)—planetary stakes rooted in a single watershed.
website
https://linktr.ee/thedbc
bioregional_context
This bioregion encompasses the area from Chirripó (Costa Rica's highest point) through the Térraba-Sierpe wetlands to the Marino Ballena National Park. The region holds approximately half a million different species—5% of the world's biodiversity—concentrated in just 0.03% of Earth's land mass.
category
